name: 声音的产生与传播
subject: physics
curriculum_points: 2.3.1 通过实验，认识声的产生和传播条件。
例1 在鼓面上放碎纸屑，敲击鼓面，观察纸屑的运动；拨动张紧的橡皮筋，观察与其接触的物体的运动。了解实验中将微小变化放大的方法。
例2 将发声器放入玻璃罩中，逐渐抽出罩内空气，会听到发声器发出的声音逐渐变小，分析导致该现象的原因。

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 声音的产生与传播

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 物理
- **学段**: 初中
- **年级**: 八年级
- **章节**: 声学
- **知识点**: 声音的产生与传播
- **课标版本**: 义务教育课程标准（2022年版）

---

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
>
2.3.1 通过实验，认识声的产生和传播条件。

例1 在鼓面上放碎纸屑，敲击鼓面，观察纸屑的运动；敲击音叉，观察与其接触的物体的运动。了解实验中将微小变化放大的方法。

例2 将发声器放入玻璃罩中，逐渐抽出罩内空气，会听到发声器发出的声音逐渐变小，分析导致该现象的原因。
>
---
>
### 1.2 学业要求
>
同机械运动学业要求
>
---
>
### 1.3 教学提示
>
同机械运动教学提示
---

## 二、教学目标

### 2.1 知识与技能
- 学生能够理解声音的产生与传播的基本概念和原理
- 学生能够正确进行相关计算和推理
- 学生能够识别和描述相关的概念、现象或材料
- 学生能够理解声音的产生与传播的基本概念和原理

### 2.2 过程与方法
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习物理的方法
- 学生能够运用物理学科方法解决问题
- 学生能够在交流中表达自己的思路
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习物理的方法

### 2.3 情感态度与价值观
- 学生感受物理与现实生活密切相关
- 学生体验物理探究的乐趣，树立学好物理的信心
- 学生养成认真细致、独立思考的学习习惯
- 学生感受物理与现实生活密切相关

---

## 三、教学重难点

### 3.1 教学重点
- 声音的产生与传播的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构
- 声音的产生与传播的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构

### 3.2 教学难点
- 掌握声音的产生与传播的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用
- 掌握声音的产生与传播的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用

---

## 五、教学建议（学科化补灌）

### 5.1 教学方法
- 以可观察实验或生活现象引入「声音的产生与传播」，先让学生描述现象，再提出可检验的问题。
- 强调控制变量、数据记录、图像分析和单位意识，避免只记公式不理解条件。
- 围绕课标要点“2.3.1 通过实验，认识声的产生和传播条件。”，设计预测—实验—解释—迁移的学习链条。

deep_textbook_snippets.text: Chapter 13 • Waves and Their Properties Access for free at openstax.org. > **[Figure 223, Page 404]** A figure or diagram (488×225px) related to: Types, Waves, Section, Learning, Objectives, section. This image appears on page 404 of the textbook. ![Figure 223 - A figure or diagram (488×225px) related to: Types, Waves, Se](OpenStax_HighSchool_Physics_images/img_0223_c8551e7f.png) Longitudinal Waves and Transverse Waves Mechanical waves are categorized by their type of motion and fall into any of two categories: transverse or longitudinal. Note that both transverse and longitudinal waves can be periodic. A transverse wave propagates so that the disturbance is perpendicular to the direction of propagation. An example of a transverse wave is shown in Figure 13.3, where a woman moves a toy spring up and down, generating waves that propagate away from herself in the horizontal direction while disturbing the toy spring in the vertical direction. Figure 13.3 In this example of a transverse wave, the wave propagates horizontally and the disturbance in the toy spring is in the vertical direction. In contrast, in a longitudinal wave, the disturbance is parallel to the direction of propagation. Figure 13.4 shows an example of a longitudinal wave, where the woman now creates a disturbance in the horizontal direction—which is the same direction as the wave propagation—by stretching and then compressing the toy spring. Figure 13.4 In this example of a longitudinal wave, the wave propagates horizontally and the disturbance in the toy spring is also in the horizontal direction.

> **[Figure 245, Page 433]** A figure or diagram (300×234px) related to: Relationship, Speed, Sound, Frequency, Wavelength, Wave. This image appears on page 433 of the textbook. ![Figure 245 - A figure or diagram (300×234px) related to: Relationship, Sp](OpenStax_HighSchool_Physics_images/img_0245_c8551e7f.png) > **[Figure 246, Page 433]** A figure or diagram (298×164px) related to: Relationship, Speed, Sound, Frequency, Wavelength, Wave. This image appears on page 433 of the textbook. ![Figure 246 - A figure or diagram (298×164px) related to: Relationship, Sp](OpenStax_HighSchool_Physics_images/img_0246_c8551e7f.png) This simulation lets you see sound waves. Adjust the frequency or amplitude (volume) and you can see and hear how the wave changes. Move the listener around and hear what she hears. Switch to the Two Source Interference tab or the Interference by Reflection tab to experiment with interference and reflection. ## Tips For Success Make sure to have audio enabled and set to Listener rather than Speaker, or else the sound will not vary as you move the listener around.

## Strategy We again use Faraday’s law of induction, although this time the magnetic field is constant and the area enclosed by the circuit changes. The circuit contains a single loop, so The rate of change of the area is Thus the rate of change of the magnetic flux is where we have used the fact that the angle between the area vector and the magnetic field is 0°. Once we know the emf, we can find the current by using Ohm’s law. To find the direction of the current, we apply Lenz’s law. Solution Faraday’s law of induction gives Solving Ohm’s law for the current and using the previous result for emf gives As the rod slides to the right, the magnetic flux passing through the circuit increases. Lenz’s law tells us that the current induced will create a magnetic field that will counter this increase. Thus, the magnetic field created by the induced current must be into the page. Curling your right-hand fingers around the loop in the clockwise direction makes your right thumb point into the page, which is the desired direction of the magnetic field. Thus, the current must flow in the clockwise direction around the circuit. Discussion Is energy conserved in this circuit? An external agent must pull on the rod with sufficient force to just balance the force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field—recall that The rate at which this force does work on the rod should be balanced by the rate at which the circuit dissipates power. Using the force required to pull the wire at a constant speed v is where we used the fact that the angle between the current and the magnetic field is
